denial_pattern_sources: missing_erisa_disclosures: DOL EBSA Technical Release 2011-01; common in Aetna pre-2024 templates
unreasonable_documentation_deadline: DOL ERISA Claims Procedure Regulations 29 CFR § 2560.503-1(g); AMA 2023 Prior Authorization Survey
denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We have completed our review of the request for Intensive outpatient program (IOP) for AUD related to Severe alcohol use disorder (F10.20).

After clinical review using Cigna Medical Coverage Policies and MCG Care Guidelines, we are unable to approve coverage at this time.

Determination summary: Outpatient counseling is sufficient; IOP level not medically necessary. Our records were reviewed against the applicable utilization-management criteria for medical necessity determinations. Specifically, this review evaluated whether the requested service meets the plan's definition of medical necessity and whether required administrative steps were completed. Insurer claims a lower level of care suffices (IOP→outpatient, inpatient→IOP).

Clinical review notes indicate the documentation submitted does not satisfy the criteria required to authorize Intensive outpatient program (IOP) for AUD. If you believe additional information was not considered, you may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a medical director; however, any such discussion must occur within the timeframe stated in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, submit a written request with supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification, provider information, and any letters of medical necessity.

Sincerely,

Cigna Health Care Management
clinical_context:
The 63-year-old member carries a documented diagnosis of Severe alcohol use disorder (F10.20). He has been under continuous care with treating specialists who recommended Intensive outpatient program (IOP) for AUD as the next appropriate step.

ASAM criteria met for IOP; outpatient alone failed twice. Treating clinicians submitted office notes, medication trials with dates and doses, and objective findings supporting medical necessity. The insurer's letter instead relies on level of care too high without engaging the specific evidence already on file.

Contrary to the denial narrative, the chart shows prior therapies were attempted for adequate duration and failed or were contraindicated. The appeal should emphasize documented step-therapy exceptions where applicable, correct application of specialty-appropriate guidelines, and any procedural defects in the notice (for example vague policy edition citations or incomplete appeal rights). The member remains symptomatic and faces delay-related harm if the requested service is further postponed.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Letter reads as authentic Cigna correspondence with MCG Care Guidelines and procedural tone.
